content:
By Earthward Editorial Team · Earthward's crystal care and sourcing team · Updated 2026-06-28 · Reviewed by Earthward's mineral-sourcing reviewers

Amethyst is the crystal most people own first — and the one most likely to be faked. Its purple color is familiar, its popularity is enormous, and the gap between cheap glass imitations and genuine stone is wide enough to make substitution tempting. If you bought an amethyst point, a tumbled stone, or a beaded bracelet and want to know whether it is the real thing, this guide walks through seven tests that work specifically for amethyst, with the mineralogy behind each one.

Quick Answer: How to Tell If a Amethyst Is Real

Real amethyst is a purple variety of quartz (SiO₂, Mohs 7) colored by iron and natural irradiation. Because it is quartz, it shares quartz's properties: it feels cool, it is hard enough to scratch glass, and under a 10x loupe it shows natural inclusions rather than round bubbles. The tests that catch amethyst fakes specifically look at color (is it naturally zoned or uniformly saturated?), inclusions (needles and two-phase pockets, or synthetic "breadcrumbs"?), and fluorescence (synthetic amethyst often glows blue under UV). Run them together and the picture gets clear.

The three most reliable at-home tests to start with:

  • Temperature — genuine amethyst (quartz, Mohs 7) feels cool and stays cool in your hand, because minerals conduct heat faster than glass or plastic.
  • Color zoning — natural amethyst shows subtle lighter and darker purple gradients, concentrated at the crystal tips. Synthetic amethyst looks uniformly saturated and "too perfect."
  • Price and naming — a large "natural amethyst" geode at the price of a tumbled stone is a red flag, and a listing that calls it "purple crystal" instead of naming the mineral deserves scrutiny.

No single home test is 100% conclusive — these tests work together. For valuable specimens, a gemological lab report is the gold standard.

Why Fake Crystals Are So Common

Amethyst is the most faked crystal in the world for a straightforward reason: it is the most popular. High demand, finite natural supply, and a purple color that glass and lab processes can imitate closely create a strong incentive to substitute. Synthetic amethyst has been manufactured since the 1970s and is chemically identical to natural amethyst — same SiO₂, same Mohs 7, same density. Glass dyed purple is even cheaper. Together, these imitations fill a large share of the market, especially in inexpensive beads and small decorative pieces.

The fakes fall into three main groups. Glass dyed purple is the cheapest — often sold as tumbled stones or beads, recognizable by round bubbles under a loupe and a too-uniform color. Synthetic (lab-grown) amethyst is chemically genuine quartz but factory-made, usually identifiable by its uniform saturation, "breadcrumb" inclusions, and blue fluorescence under shortwave UV. And then there is the disclosure problem: heat-treated low-grade amethyst is often sold as "citrine" (the burnt-orange variety), which is a legitimate treatment but should be disclosed, not passed off as natural citrine.

Synthetic amethyst is genuinely difficult to distinguish from natural, because it is the same mineral. The differences are subtle: the way color is distributed, the type of inclusions, the fluorescence response. Glass is easier — bubbles, wrong density, soft enough to scratch. This is why amethyst testing leans heavily on color zoning and inclusions rather than a single "gotcha" test. A genuine natural amethyst shows the irregular signatures of slow geological growth; a factory-made stone shows the uniformity of weeks in a lab.

How to Tell If Amethyst Is Real: 7 Tests

These seven tests are ordered from least destructive and most amethyst-specific to more general confirmation. The color zoning and inclusion checks are the headline tests — they separate most natural amethyst from glass and synthetic. The temperature and hardness tests confirm the quartz family. Price and naming read the marketplace. None is conclusive alone; together they tell a clear story.

🌈 Test 5: Color Banding & Zoning

The science behind it: Natural color in minerals forms unevenly because crystal growth happens over geological time under changing conditions. Genuine amethyst shows subtle color zoning — deeper purple at the tips, lighter near the base, with natural gradation. Synthetic quartz grown in a lab colors uniformly and looks "flat" or overly saturated. Dyed stones concentrate pigment in surface cracks and fissures rather than throughout.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Color distributionUneven zoning, natural gradationUniform, flat, "too perfect"
Where color sitsThroughout the crystal, following growth structureDyed: concentrated in cracks/fissures; synthetic: perfectly even
SaturationSubtle variation, lighter and darker zonesSingle flat tone, often over-saturated

How to do it:

  1. Look at the stone under daylight (not yellow indoor light, which distorts color).
  2. Examine whether the color is uniform or shows natural lighter/darker zones — genuine amethyst, citrine, and fluorite almost always show some variation.
  3. For suspected dye: check cracks and chips — dye pools in surface fissures and looks more intense there.

Limitations: Some high-quality natural stones are genuinely uniform in color, and some synthetic stones are deliberately made with simulated zoning. So uniform color is a flag, not a verdict — combine with other tests. Dye tests can fade over time; a cotton swab dampened with acetone (for non-quartz stones) can lift surface dye, but this is mildly destructive.

Best stones for this test: Amethyst (most classic case), citrine, fluorite, agate, rose quartz, lapis lazuli. Not useful for naturally uniform stones or opaque dyed stones without cracks to inspect.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz color zoning; mindat.org — amethyst color cause (iron + irradiation).

🔍 Test 4: Bubble Inspection (10x Loupe)

The science behind it: Manufactured glass traps small spherical or teardrop-shaped air bubbles as it cools — a tell-tale sign of industrial origin. Natural minerals form over geological time and instead contain natural inclusions: needle-like rutile, two-phase gas-liquid inclusions, mineral crystals, or "fingerprints" of healed fractures. A 10x jeweler's loupe reveals the difference: round bubbles mean glass; irregular inclusions mean natural.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Under 10x loupeNeedle/plate inclusions, growth lines, mineral flecksPerfectly round bubbles, swirls, no irregular inclusions
Bubble shapeNone (natural minerals rarely trap air)Spherical or teardrop bubbles
Internal textureChaotic, asymmetric, "natural"Too clean, or with flow lines from manufacturing

How to do it:

  1. Use a 10x jeweler's loupe (an inexpensive tool — about $10-15 online).
  2. Hold the stone against a light source (window or lamp) and look through it with the loupe.
  3. Scan for perfectly round, spherical bubbles — these are characteristic of glass. Natural stones show irregular inclusions instead.

Limitations: High-quality optical glass and fused quartz can be manufactured bubble-free, so the absence of bubbles does not guarantee natural origin — but the presence of round bubbles almost always indicates glass. Some natural stones (like enhydro quartz) do contain moving water bubbles, but these are elongated or angular, not perfectly round. Requires a loupe; the naked eye usually cannot resolve the smallest bubbles.

Best stones for this test: Transparent stones sold as clear quartz, citrine, topaz, or diamond simulants. Less useful for opaque stones where you cannot see inside.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — inclusions in natural vs. synthetic; mindat.org.

🟣 Test 6: UV Light (Fluorescence) Test

The science behind it: Some minerals fluoresce — emit visible light — under ultraviolet (UV) radiation, due to trace impurities called activators. Natural fluorite often fluoresces blue or purple; genuine scapolite glows yellow-orange; some natural quartz shows weak fluorescence. The fluorescence pattern of a natural stone differs from glass (usually inert) and from some synthetics (which may fluoresce differently). A 365nm UV flashlight reveals this.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Under 365nm UV lightSpecific fluorescence (e.g., fluorite → blue/purple)Glass usually inert; some synthetics fluoresce abnormally
Fluorescence patternPatchy, follows natural growth zonesUniform or absent (glass), or too uniform (some synthetics)
Color of glowSpecies-specific (scapolite yellow, fluorite blue)None, or wrong color for the claimed species

How to do it:

  1. Use a 365nm UV flashlight (about $10-15 online; the shorter 395nm "blacklight" is less precise).
  2. Darken the room as much as possible.
  3. Shine the UV on the stone and observe any glow — note the color and whether it is patchy or uniform.

Limitations: Not all minerals fluoresce — many genuine stones (including most clear quartz) show no reaction, so a negative result does not mean fake. Fluorescence also varies even within a single mineral species depending on impurities. This test is most useful as confirmation for known fluorescent species (fluorite, scapolite, some calcite, some amber). Requires a UV light source and a dark room.

Best stones for this test: Fluorite (reliable blue/purple), scapolite, amber (fluoresces blue-green), some calcite. Not useful as a general test for non-fluorescent species like clear quartz.

Source: mindat.org — mineral fluorescence data; GIA Gem Reference Guide.

🌡️ Test 1: Temperature Test

The science behind it: Minerals transfer heat faster than glass or plastic because their ordered crystalline lattice conducts thermal energy more efficiently than the disordered amorphous structure of glass. A natural crystal at room temperature (around 20°C) therefore feels noticeably cool when first touched and stays cool longer, while glass and plastic — which conduct heat poorly — quickly warm to skin temperature.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Initial touchNoticeably coolCloser to room temperature
After 10 seconds in handStill coolWarmed up
Thermal conductivityHigh (crystal lattice)Low (amorphous glass / polymer)

How to do it:

  1. Hold the stone in your palm or against your inner wrist (more temperature-sensitive than fingertips) for about 10 seconds.
  2. Compare the feel against a known glass object of similar size (a glass bead or marble) at the same room temperature.
  3. A genuine quartz, amethyst, or citrine keeps a cool edge; glass warms quickly.

Limitations: Room temperature, body heat, and ambient humidity all affect the result — test in a neutral 20°C room, not after the stone sat in sunlight or a hot car. Small beads (under 8mm) are harder to judge by touch because they warm up faster regardless of material. This is a useful first signal, never a standalone verdict.

Best stones for this test: Quartz family (clear quartz, amethyst, citrine, rose quartz, smoky quartz), topaz. Less reliable for opaque stones (jasper, howlite) where surface texture masks the temperature cue.

Source: mindat.org — mineral thermal conductivity; GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz identification.

🔨 Test 2: Hardness / Scratch Test

The science behind it: The Mohs scale ranks minerals by scratch resistance from 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond). Genuine quartz sits at Mohs 7, meaning it can scratch ordinary window glass (about Mohs 5.5) and steel (around Mohs 6). Glass imitations, being softer or equally soft, cannot reliably scratch true glass. The test exploits this hardness gap.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Scratching glassQuartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass easilyGlass (Mohs 5.5) cannot scratch glass
Being scratched by steelSteel (Mohs 6) cannot scratch quartzSteel can scratch glass imitation
Edge after scratchLeaves a clear grooveNo mark, or only crumbles

How to do it:

  1. Take a piece of ordinary window glass (a glass jar you do not mind damaging, never good glassware).
  2. Press a sharp edge or point of the stone firmly and drag it across the glass.
  3. A genuine quartz leaves a visible scratch groove; a glass imitation leaves no mark or only a faint streak that rubs off.

Limitations: This is a destructive test — it damages both the stone (edge) and the glass. Never use it on finished jewelry, polished pieces, or stones you want to keep pristine. It does not work for genuine soft stones (malachite Mohs 3.5-4, calcite Mohs 3, turquoise Mohs 5-6) which legitimately cannot scratch glass. Use only as a confirmation on quartz-family rough or tumbled stones.

Best stones for this test: Quartz family (Mohs 7), topaz (Mohs 8), jadeite (Mohs 6.5-7). Not suitable for soft stones, jewelry, or polished specimens.

Source: mindat.org — Mohs hardness scale; USGS Mineral Database — quartz hardness.

💰 Test 9: Price Red Flag

The science behind it: Genuine natural crystals carry real costs: mining, sorting, cutting, polishing, transport, and the reality that high-quality specimens are finite. A price far below the established market range signals that something is off — either synthetic, glass, dyed, or reconstituted material being sold as natural. While promotions exist, a consistent dramatic discount across a seller's entire catalog is a structural red flag, not a sale.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Price vs. market rangeWithin reasonable market range for the species and qualityFar below market (often 50-80% cheaper)
Consistency of discountOccasional promotions on specific piecesEntire catalog suspiciously cheap
Quality claims vs. pricePrice matches claimed grade"Museum quality" at bargain prices

How to do it:

  1. Compare the seller's prices against established reference points (gemological market guides, reputable dealers).
  2. Be wary of "too good to be true" pricing — a large natural amethyst geode for the price of a tumbled stone is not a deal, it is a different material.
  3. Use price as one signal among many — a fair price does not guarantee authenticity, but a wildly low price is a strong warning.

Limitations: Price alone never confirms authenticity — a seller can charge full price for glass. And genuine inexpensive stones exist (clear quartz, common agate, rose quartz rough are legitimately affordable). Use price as an auxiliary signal: a red flag warrants deeper testing, but a reasonable price does not exempt a stone from the other tests.

Best stones for this test: All stones, as an auxiliary signal — especially high-value species (jadeite, moldavite, large amethyst geodes, tanzanite) where prices are well-established and dramatic discounts are implausible.

Source: market reference — gemological pricing guides.

🏷️ Test 10: Naming & Source Transparency

The science behind it: Reputable sellers identify stones by their proper mineral name and origin, because mineral species and locality affect value and care. Vague or misleading names are a marketing red flag: the glass industry calls leaded glass "crystal" (lead crystal, crystal glass), so "crystal quartz" can mean glass rather than natural quartz. Sellers who hide behind "mystery stone," "energy crystal," or omit locality are harder to hold accountable.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Stone name on listingProper mineral name (Natural Amethyst, SiO₂)Vague ("purple crystal," "energy stone") or industry code ("crystal quartz" = glass)
Treatment disclosureDisclosed (heat-treated citrine, lab-created)Hidden or denied
LocalitySpecific origin (Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia)Generic ("imported," none listed)

How to do it:

  1. Read the listing carefully — does it name the actual mineral species, or use vague marketing terms?
  2. Check for treatment disclosure: reputable sellers note heat treatment, irradiation, dyeing, or lab creation.
  3. Look for locality information — specific origins (Brazil for amethyst, Madagascar for labradorite) signal a seller who knows their stock.

Limitations: A seller who names stones correctly can still sell glass; and a small honest seller may omit locality without ill intent. Naming transparency is a positive signal but not proof. Conversely, vague naming is a warning, not a verdict. Use it to decide which sellers warrant deeper trust, combined with the other tests.

Best stones for this test: All stones, especially when buying online sight-unseen. The single most useful pre-purchase signal.

Source: GIA / FTC guidelines on gemstone disclosure.

Amethyst: Deep Dive (Bracelets, Beads, and Geodes)

Amethyst's mineral profile is the foundation for every test above. Here is the quick reference, then the specifics for the three forms amethyst most commonly takes — bracelets and beads, tumbled stones, and geodes — plus the price ranges that help you spot a "too good to be true" listing.

Amethyst: Quick Mineral Profile

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544 · Color cause: Iron + natural irradiation. Primary origins: Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia, Mexico.

What gets faked:

  • Synthetic (lab-grown) purple quartz
  • Glass dyed purple
  • Heat-treated low-grade amethyst sold as "citrine"

How to tell Amethyst is real — the key tests:

  1. Color zoning is the headline test — natural amethyst shows subtle lighter/darker purple gradients, especially concentrated at crystal tips. Synthetic amethyst looks uniformly saturated and "too perfect."
  2. Inclusions under 10x loupe: natural amethyst has needle-like rutile, two-phase gas-liquid inclusions, or "fingerprints." Synthetic amethyst often shows "breadcrumb" inclusions from lab seed-crystal growth.
  3. UV fluorescence: synthetic amethyst frequently shows a blue fluorescence under shortwave UV; natural amethyst is usually inert or shows weak reddish-brown.
  4. Pricing sanity check: a large "natural amethyst" geode at the price of a tumbled stone is almost certainly dyed or reconstituted.

Price sanity check: Tumbled stones $2-8; beads $0.50-3 each; small geodes $15-60; large cathedral geodes $100-2000+ depending on size and quality

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz; mindat.org — amethyst (Fe³⁺ + irradiation color cause).

Read the full Amethyst meaning · Shop Amethyst

For the full 10-test overview across all crystal types, start with How to Tell If a Crystal Is Real (full 10-test guide).

See the full list: Most Faked Crystals: the full list.

When to Use Professional Tools

Most amethyst tests need only your eyes, your hands, and a $10 loupe. Two additional tools — a UV light and, for valuable geodes, a gemological lab — raise your confidence considerably.

  • A 10x jeweler's loupe is the single most useful tool for amethyst. It reveals whether the color is zoned or uniform, whether the inclusions are natural needles or synthetic "breadcrumbs," and whether there are round bubbles (glass) hiding inside. For amethyst beads and small tumbled stones, the loupe is where the answer usually lives.
  • A Mohs hardness reference (or a piece of ordinary glass) confirms the quartz family on rough stones: amethyst (Mohs 7) scratches glass easily. Use only on rough or stones you do not mind marking — never on a polished bracelet or finished jewelry. For jewelry, the temperature and visual tests are safer.
  • A 365nm UV flashlight is the best tool for separating synthetic from natural amethyst. Synthetic amethyst frequently shows a blue fluorescence under shortwave UV; natural amethyst is usually inert or shows a weak reddish-brown. It is a confirmation test, not a standalone verdict — combine with color zoning and inclusions.
  • A gemological refractometer confirms the quartz reading (RI 1.544) precisely. Since both natural and synthetic amethyst share this reading, a refractometer confirms "quartz" but does not alone distinguish natural from lab-grown — for that, inclusions and fluorescence matter more.
  • For valuable amethyst — large geodes, cathedral points, or specimens sold as fine-quality — a gemological laboratory report is the definitive answer. Labs can distinguish natural from synthetic amethyst through inclusion analysis and spectroscopy that no home test matches.

For high-value specimens (jadeite, imperial-grade amethyst, moldavite, large geodes), a gemological laboratory certificate is the only definitive answer — home tests narrow the odds, they do not replace a professional report.

Frequently Asked Questions About Real vs Fake Amethyst

How can you tell if amethyst is real or fake?

Run several tests together, with color zoning and the inclusion check leading. Genuine amethyst shows natural purple gradients — lighter and darker zones, often concentrated at the crystal tips — and under a 10x loupe it contains irregular inclusions (needle rutile, two-phase gas-liquid pockets) rather than round bubbles. It feels cool to the touch (quartz conducts heat well), and on rough stones it scratches glass (Mohs 7). If the color is suspiciously uniform, the inclusions look like "breadcrumbs," or it fluoresces blue under UV, it may be synthetic. A genuine natural amethyst passes most of these; a fake fails several.

What is the most reliable test for real amethyst?

The color zoning test combined with the inclusion check is the most reliable starting pair for amethyst. Natural amethyst almost always shows some color variation — subtle gradients of lighter and darker purple. Under a 10x loupe, look for needle-like inclusions, two-phase pockets, or "fingerprints" of healed fractures. Synthetic amethyst tends to be uniformly saturated with "breadcrumb" inclusions; glass shows round bubbles. Add a UV check (synthetic often fluoresces blue) and the picture is usually clear enough for a confident home verdict.

How can I tell if my amethyst bracelet is real?

For an amethyst bracelet, start with non-destructive tests since you cannot (and should not) scratch the beads. Look at the color: do the beads show natural variation between them, or are they all an identical flat purple? Genuine amethyst beads vary slightly in tone; glass beads are often identical. Hold a bead against your inner wrist — it should feel cool and stay cool. Use a 10x loupe on a bead: natural amethyst shows irregular inclusions; glass shows round bubbles. The price is a signal too — a strand of large "amethyst" beads for a few dollars is very likely glass or dyed.

Does real amethyst have bubbles?

No — and this is one of the clearest signals. Natural amethyst, like all natural quartz, contains irregular inclusions (needles, veils, mineral flecks) or is inclusion-free, but it does not contain round air bubbles. Round bubbles are a signature of manufactured glass, trapped as the glass cools. So if you see perfectly round bubbles inside a "purple crystal" under a 10x loupe, it is almost certainly glass, not natural amethyst. The one exception is rare "enhydro" quartz, which contains water — but those inclusions are elongated or angular, not perfectly round.

Where can I buy genuine amethyst?

Look for sellers who name the stone as "Natural Amethyst" or "Amethyst (SiO₂)," disclose any treatments, and state an origin (Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia, Mexico are common amethyst sources). Reputable sellers provide clear photos showing natural color variation between pieces, offer a return policy, and often share mineral data. The amethyst collection on this site names stones and sources for this reason — so you can verify what you are buying rather than trusting a vague label.

How much should real amethyst cost?

Prices vary by form and quality. A few reference points: tumbled amethyst stones run $2-8; individual beads $0.50-3 each; small points $5-15; small geodes $15-60; large cathedral geodes $100 to over $2000 depending on size, color depth, and crystal formation. The useful signal is not the absolute price but the ratio — a large "natural amethyst geode" at the price of a tumbled stone is almost certainly dyed glass or reconstituted material. Learn the rough range for the form you want, and treat prices far below it as a reason to test carefully.

Are lab-created amethyst stones real?

Lab-created (synthetic) amethyst is genuinely amethyst in composition — it is SiO₂, colored by iron, with the same Mohs 7 hardness and density as natural. What it is not is natural: it was grown in a factory over weeks rather than mined after forming over geological time. The material is real; the question is disclosure. Selling synthetic amethyst as natural, without labeling it "lab-created," is misleading and against FTC guidelines. If you want a natural specimen — for the inclusions, the growth history, or the symbolic connection to geological time — ask explicitly and read the listing for "lab-created" or "synthetic."

Can home tests guarantee amethyst is real?

No — and this is especially true for amethyst, because synthetic amethyst is chemically identical to natural. Home tests (color zoning, inclusions, fluorescence) catch most glass fakes and many synthetics, but a well-made synthetic can pass several tests. For a valuable amethyst geode or a fine specimen, the only definitive answer distinguishing natural from synthetic is a gemological laboratory report, which uses inclusion analysis and spectroscopy. Home tests are a strong first filter; they are not a final verdict on expensive pieces.

Why is my amethyst losing color?

Color change in amethyst usually points to one of three causes. First, prolonged direct sunlight can fade amethyst's purple — the color comes from iron and natural irradiation, and UV exposure can lighten it over time. Keep amethyst out of direct sun. Second, some "amethyst" sold cheaply is dyed, and dye can bleed or fade with washing or wear — look for dye concentrated in cracks under a loupe. Third, heat turns amethyst into citrine (yellow/orange) — this is how most commercial "citrine" is made, a legitimate treatment but one that should be disclosed. If your amethyst is shifting toward yellow or brown, it may have been heat-sensitive or dyed.

Is dark amethyst better than light amethyst?

Not necessarily — and the assumption that darker is better drives a lot of fakery. Natural amethyst ranges from pale lavender to deep reddish-purple. The most prized color is a medium-deep purple with subtle red flashes (often associated with Siberian or Uruguayan sources), but pale amethyst is still genuinely amethyst. What matters is whether the color looks natural — with zoning and variation — rather than artificially uniform and oversaturated. Some of the darkest, most uniform "amethyst" on the market is synthetic or dyed, chosen precisely because buyers equate dark with valuable. Judge color by whether it looks naturally grown, not by depth alone.

Amethyst identification tests combine practical mineralogy (color zoning, inclusions, fluorescence) with marketplace awareness. Home tests help you spot common fakes, but for valuable amethyst geodes, a gemological lab report is the gold standard. Crystal meanings and energetic properties are separate from authenticity.

How to Buy Genuine Crystals (Without Getting Fooled)

If the tests above tell you how to read an amethyst, this section tells you how to read the seller. Amethyst is one of the most faked crystals, so the seller's transparency matters as much as the stone's properties. The five signs below consistently separate sellers who know their amethyst from those who do not.

Five Signs of a Reputable Seller

  • Naming transparency. The listing names the stone as "Natural Amethyst" or "Amethyst (SiO₂)" — not vague terms like "purple crystal." Treatments are disclosed: heat-treated amethyst sold as citrine, lab-created synthetic amethyst, dyed material. A seller who names amethyst correctly and discloses treatments is signaling they know the difference.
  • Locality disclosure. The listing states a specific origin — Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia, or Mexico are common amethyst sources. Specific origins matter because they affect both value and appearance (Siberian and Uruguayan amethyst, for instance, are known for deeper color). "Imported" or no locality is not proof of a fake, but it is a missed signal.
  • Mineral data availability. The seller provides the Mohs hardness (7), specific gravity (2.65), or refractive index (1.544), or links to it. This signals a seller who understands mineralogy and lets you cross-check the home tests against the known values for quartz.
  • Clear return policy. A 30-day return policy lets you run the color zoning, inclusion, and UV tests at home and send the amethyst back if it fails. Sellers who move fakes resist returns; reputable sellers welcome them.
  • Real photographs. The listing shows the actual amethyst piece you will receive, with its natural color zoning and inclusions visible — not identical catalog shots across hundreds of listings. Genuine amethyst varies from piece to piece; mass-produced fakes look uniform.

How Earthward Approaches This

At Earthward, we name each amethyst by its mineral species, state the origin where we know it, and disclose any treatments honestly. We let the stones show their natural color zoning and inclusions rather than presenting an artificially uniform "perfect" purple. "Return to what's real" means we tell you what you are holding — a natural stone with its own character, not a vague label.
